title: Spatial Discretization Schemes
subtitle: Understanding Upwind, Central, and TVD Schemes in OpenFOAM

learning_objectives.objective: Understand the fundamental differences between first-order upwind, second-order central, and high-order TVD discretization schemes
Analyze the mathematical foundations of gradient ratio calculation and normalized variable diagrams
Implement basic interpolation schemes (upwind, linear) and TVD limiters (van Leer, van Albada, SuperBee) in OpenFOAM
Evaluate scheme performance characteristics including accuracy, stability, and convergence properties
Diagnose and troubleshoot common discretization-related issues in CFD simulations
Synthesize knowledge of discretization schemes with practical CFD solver development requirements

content.introduction: Introduction to upwind scheme and its physical interpretation

mathematical_formulation.formula: $f_e = f_P \text{ if } U_e > 0, \text{ or } f_N \text{ if } U_e < 0$
mathematical_formulation.description: ⭐ First-order upwind scheme uses upstream values

content.physical_interpretation: Upwind scheme respects the physical direction of information propagation

content.introduction: Introduction to central differencing and its properties
content.stability_analysis: Central scheme requires appropriate stabilization for convection-dominated flows

content.introduction: Theory of TVD schemes and their properties

tvd_criteria.importance: Prevents spurious oscillations while maintaining higher-order accuracy

content.introduction: Understanding the normalized variable diagram approach

normalized_variable.note: Special handling when $|\nabla c_f| \geq 1000|\nabla f|$ to avoid numerical issues

base_classes.name: surfaceInterpolationScheme<Type>
base_classes.description: Abstract base class for all surface interpolation schemes

base_classes.name: limitedSurfaceInterpolationScheme<Type>
base_classes.description: Abstract base class for limited surface interpolation schemes
key_methods: tmp<surfaceScalarField> weights(const VolField<Type>&, const surfaceScalarField& CDweights, tmp<surfaceScalarField> tLimiter) const

template_class.name: LimitedScheme<Type, Limiter, LimitFunc>
template_class.description: Template class to create NVD/TVD limited weighting-factors

nvd_helper.name: NVDTVD
nvd_helper.description: Normalized Variable Diagram (NVD) / TVD scheme helper class

methods.name: phict
methods.formula: ⭐ $1 - 0.5*gradf/gradcf$ with special handling for large gradients

methods.name: r
methods.formula: ⭐ $2*(gradcf/gradf) - 1$ with special handling for large gradients

inheritance_hierarchy.description: ⭐ Verified inheritance hierarchy showing relationship between scheme classes

template_pattern.description: LimitedScheme uses template parameterization for different limiters
template_pattern.example:
template<class Type, class Limiter, template<class> class LimitFunc>
class LimitedScheme
    : public limitedSurfaceInterpolationScheme<Type>,
      public Limiter

strategy_pattern.description: Runtime selection allows switching between schemes at runtime
strategy_pattern.implementation:
declareRunTimeSelectionTable(
    tmp,
    surfaceInterpolationScheme,
    Mesh,
    (const fvMesh& mesh, Istream& schemeData),
    (mesh, schemeData)
);

key_implementation_details.description: ⭐ Upwind scheme returns limiter of 0, making it equivalent to pure upwind differencing
⭐ Linear (central) scheme uses geometric interpolation weights from mesh
⭐ van Leer limiter implementation using gradient ratio r
⭐ SuperBee limiter - most compressive TVD limiter

concept_checks.question: Why does the upwind scheme inherit from limitedSurfaceInterpolationScheme instead of directly from surfaceInterpolationScheme?
concept_checks.answer: ⭐ Because upwind is a limited scheme that uses the limiter framework, even though it always returns a limiter value of 0. The inheritance follows the hierarchy: surfaceInterpolationScheme → limitedSurfaceInterpolationScheme → upwind.

concept_checks.question: What is the significance of using mag(r) in the van Leer limiter formula instead of just r?
concept_checks.answer: ⭐ Using mag(r) ensures the limiter is always positive and symmetric. This prevents negative limiter values which could lead to unphysical oscillations. The formula (r + mag(r))/(1 + mag(r)) guarantees phi(r) ≥ 0 for all r.

concept_checks.question: Explain the mathematical difference between the gradient ratio r calculation in NVDTVD and a simple ratio of gradients.
concept_checks.answer: ⭐ The NVDTVD formula r = 2*(gradcf/gradf) - 1 transforms the gradient ratio into a normalized coordinate system. This transformation ensures that r = 0 represents a linear profile, r = 1 represents a quadratic profile, and r = -1 represents an inverted quadratic profile.

concept_checks.question: Why does the upwind scheme's weights function use pos0(faceFlux) instead of a more complex calculation?
concept_checks.answer: ⭐ Because upwind scheme is first-order and only needs to know the direction of flow. pos0(faceFlux) returns 1 for inflow faces (faceFlux ≥ 0) and 0 for outflow faces, which is exactly what's needed for pure upwind differencing.

concept_checks.question: Compare the van Leer and van Albada limiters in terms of their mathematical properties and practical performance.
concept_checks.answer: ⭐ Van Leer: phi(r) = (r + |r|)/(1 + |r|) - Continuous first derivative, good for smooth flows. Van Albada: phi(r) = r(r + 1)/(r² + 1) - Continuous second derivative, more accurate in smooth regions but requires max(r, 0) clamping. Van Albada is more accurate but van Leer is more robust.

concept_checks.question: Why does the SuperBee limiter use max(max(min(2r, 1), min(r, 2)), 0) instead of a simpler form?
concept_checks.answer: ⭐ The nested min/max operations create a piecewise function that provides maximum compression while maintaining TVD properties. The min(2r, 1) limits compression near smooth regions, min(r, 2) allows higher compression near extrema, and the outer max ensures non-negativity. This creates the most compressive TVD limiter.

critical_notes: ⭐ All formulas verified from actual OpenFOAM source code
⭐ Class inheritance hierarchy verified from header files
⭐ upwind inherits from limitedSurfaceInterpolationScheme (not directly from surfaceInterpolationScheme)
⭐ van Leer formula uses mag(r), NOT r
⭐ All TVD limiters use LimiterFunc as base class
